title: Soft start morning meditation
subtitle: A five-minute body-based practice for starting the day without activating the stress response.
excerpt: A short morning meditation drawn from the SLEEP program's Day 21 theme. The practice gives the nervous system a chance to arrive in the day gently, before the first demand is made.
sourceNotes: Inspired by Day 21 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025). Body-based wake-up practices draw on body-scan lineage from mindfulness-based stress reduction (Jon Kabat-Zinn, 1979) and secular morning meditation traditions.

content.text: A five-minute morning meditation from the Day 21 theme of the SLEEP program. The practice gives the body a chance to wake up gradually rather than moving straight into urgency.
Lie on your back, eyes still closed or barely open. Don't reach for your phone. Don't think about the day yet. You're just noticing where you are.
Start with three slow breaths. On each exhale, notice that you're still in bed. The pillow is under your head. The covers are there. Nothing has been asked of you yet.

Take your attention down to your feet. Feel them. Then move slowly upward, calves, knees, thighs, back, shoulders. Not checking anything for tension; just arriving. You're doing a roll call of the body before the day begins.
When you reach your chest, take one breath into it. Let it spread. Then one breath into your belly, softer than the last.
Now say to yourself, once, slowly: "I begin this day from here."
Open your eyes slowly. Take one more breath. Then get up, but not urgently. The first two minutes of the day set the pace.

Inspired by Day 21 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025). Body-scan practice draws on mindfulness-based stress reduction, developed by Jon Kabat-Zinn at the University of Massachusetts.
